title: Toeplitz Matrices and Determinants Under the Impetus of the Ising Model
speaker: Percy Deift
abstract: This is the second of two talks in which the speaker will discuss the development of the theory of Toeplitz matrices and determinants in response to questions arising in the analysis of the Ising model of statistical mechanics. The first talk will be largely historical and the second will describe the state of the art today, including recent results of the speaker and his colleagues Alexander Its and Igor Krasovsky.

proof_stages.label: Smooth strong-Szegő baseline
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls the strong Szegő limit theorem for smooth nonvanishing winding-zero symbols and its Johansson interpretation as a central limit theorem for Haar-unitary linear statistics.

proof_stages.label: Fisher-Hartwig singular symbols
proof_stages.summary: Fisher-Hartwig symbols are introduced as Toeplitz symbols allowing zeros, root singularities, jumps, and winding, with motivating sources such as eigenvalue problems and Ising correlations.

proof_stages.label: Classical one-term Fisher-Hartwig asymptotics
proof_stages.summary: The Fisher-Hartwig conjectural form is stated, including the polynomial exponent, Wiener-Hopf and Barnes G-function constants, Basor's explicit leading term, and the later Ehrhardt theorem with its seminorm restriction.

proof_stages.label: Hidden aliases and the Basor-Tracy conjecture
proof_stages.summary: Examples show that a single Toeplitz symbol can have several beta-shifted Fisher-Hartwig representations whose naive asymptotics conflict, leading to the Basor-Tracy prescription of summing the standard term over all discrete quadratic minimizers in the beta orbit.

proof_stages.label: Biorthogonal-polynomial and Riemann-Hilbert machinery
proof_stages.summary: The proof strategy introduces biorthogonal polynomials for complex Fisher-Hartwig weights, obtains their asymptotics by nonlinear steepest descent when the beta seminorm is below one, and notes determinant identities transferring such asymptotics to Hankel and Toeplitz-plus-Hankel problems.

proof_stages.label: Discrete orbit minimization by exchanges
proof_stages.summary: The discrete variational problem is solved by proving every beta orbit has minimal seminorm at most one, with a unique minimizer below one and an exchange-generated family of minimizers at the boundary value one.

proof_stages.label: Assembly of the generalized Fisher-Hartwig formula
proof_stages.summary: In the unique-minimizer case Ehrhardt's theorem applies directly, while in the boundary case one shifts endpoint betas to a subcritical representative, expresses the resulting z^l factor through an l-by-l determinant of biorthogonal polynomials, inserts their asymptotics, and recovers exactly the sum over all minimizing aliases.

proof_stages.label: Ising-driven frontiers beyond scalar Toeplitz determinants
proof_stages.summary: The conclusion explains that the next challenges are matrix-valued block Toeplitz determinants, where Widom's constant is generally not computable, and double-scaling Ising limits, where Painlevé III, connection formulae, universality, and isomonodromic tau functions enter.

examples.name: Haar-unitary linear statistic central limit theorem
examples.note: Johansson's random-matrix interpretation identifies the Toeplitz determinant with the characteristic function of a Haar-unitary linear statistic, making the strong Szegő theorem concrete as a Gaussian CLT.
examples.engagement_reason: He derives the Haar-unitary linear statistic CLT from the strong Szegő theorem by identifying its characteristic function with a Toeplitz determinant and reading off the Gaussian variance.

examples.name: Fixed-temperature Ising correlation symbols
examples.note: The below-, critical-, and above-critical Ising symbols show how Fisher-Hartwig parameters encode the famous quarter-power decay and also expose the beyond-all-orders difficulty above the critical temperature.
examples.engagement_reason: He applies the Fisher-Hartwig formula to the Ising fixed-temperature symbols, extracting the critical n^{-1/4} decay and explaining why the high-temperature/low-temperature case with beta equal to one forces vanishing of all polynomial terms.

examples.name: One-parameter jump symbol exp(-i pi t chi_upper)
examples.note: Setting t=2 makes the symbol trivial although the naive Fisher-Hartwig formula predicts decay, visibly forcing a breakdown of the single-representation asymptotic and later illustrating the alias-selection rule.
examples.engagement_reason: He explicitly analyzes the one-parameter jump symbol, computes its Fisher-Hartwig betas, tests the asymptotic formula at t=2 to get a contradiction, and later plugs in the correct beta representatives for ranges of t.
